text: The patient is positioned supine with the right arm tucked, and a bump is placed on the left side of the abdomen to elevate the spleen.

The abdomen is prepped and draped in a sterile fashion. The surgeon accesses the abdomen at Palmer's point in the left upper quadrant, places additional ports, and uses an Athenson liver retractor to facilitate an abdominal inspection.
The surgeon incises the short gastric vessels along the upper greater curvature of the stomach using a sealing device, continuing the dissection up to the angle of His.

The surgeon begins dissection at the inferior pole of the spleen, dividing the splenocolic ligament. Following this, the posterior and lateral attachments are mobilized from the inferior portion of the spleen, with careful attention to avoid injury to the spleen.

The surgeon performs dissection of loose areolar tissue using a combination of blunt dissection with a KITNER and a sealing device. With the inferior and lateral attachments already divided, attention is now focused on the superior pole of the spleen, where any remaining attachments are lysed up to the level of the hilum.

The surgeon uses vascular load staple line reinforced endogia staplers to divide the splenic hilum, completing the mobilization of the spleen. This process requires two or three staple firings to fully separate the remaining attachments.

The surgeon places the fully divided spleen into a large endocatch bag and extracts it through the left lateral port. A Kelly clamp and ring forceps are used to morselize the spleen, ensuring the bag remains intact to prevent spillage of splenic particles within the abdomen.
